personalizeWizard.exitTitle: Are you sure you want to exit setup?
personalizeWizard.exitBody: Your progress will be saved. You can pick up where you left off anytime.
personalizeWizard.dobHelper: Your age updates automatically based on this date.
personalizeWizard.retireQuestion: At what age would you like to retire?
personalizeWizard.ageInsightLead: You have {{count}} {{yearsLabel}} until retirement. Estimated year: {{year}}.
personalizeWizard.ageInsightGrowth30: a long runway for compounding and a powerful advantage for your nest egg.
personalizeWizard.ageInsightGrowth20: a strong runway to build wealth before you tap your savings.
personalizeWizard.ageInsightGrowth10: Every extra year in the market can meaningfully boost your outcome.
personalizeWizard.ageInsightGrowthShort: We'll help you make the most of the time you have.
personalizeWizard.ageInsightTail: Retiring at {{retireAge}} gives you {{count}} years of growth — {{growth}}
personalizeWizard.popularRetireSubtitle: Based on typical retirement timelines across similar plans.
personalizeWizard.ageInsightLine1: <retire>Retiring at {{age}}</retire> means you have <yl>{{years}} {{yearsLabel}}</yl> until retirement.
personalizeWizard.ageInsightBandUnder50: Great! You have a long runway for growth.
personalizeWizard.ageInsightBand50to60: You're on track. A few adjustments can improve outcomes.
personalizeWizard.ageInsightBandOver60: Consider optimizing contributions for retirement readiness.
personalizeWizard.locationInsightFlorida: <st>Florida</st> is a popular retirement destination. With no state income tax and warm weather year-round, you could save $15,000+ annually on taxes alone.
personalizeWizard.locationInsightArizona: <st>Arizona</st> is a retiree favorite for dry sunshine and active communities. Costs vary by city, but many areas still offer strong value next to pricier coastal markets.
personalizeWizard.locationInsightNorthCarolina: <st>North Carolina</st> blends mountains, coast, and mild seasons with a moderate cost of living — a balanced mix of lifestyle and affordability.
personalizeWizard.locationInsightSouthCarolina: <st>South Carolina</st> pairs coastal charm with property taxes that are often lower than many coastal peers — a compelling place to stretch retirement income.
personalizeWizard.savingsWizardBody0: Every dollar counts! With {{amount}} saved and {{years}} {{yearsLabel}} until retirement, consistent contributions can grow this significantly through compound interest.
personalizeWizard.savingsWizardBodyMid: Nice progress — keep contributing when you can.
personalizeWizard.savingsFigmaHint: Exclude 401(k), IRA, pension — only include personal savings and investments.
personalizeWizard.savingsSubtitle: Sharing this helps us give you a clearer picture of your future.
personalizeWizard.savingsHelperHasValue: Great — we'll include this in your long-term projection.
personalizeWizard.savingsHelperEmpty: No problem — we'll plan based on this account alone.
personalizeWizard.savingsInsightZero: Starting from $0 is common — your plan contributions will build your balance over time. Even small amounts compound meaningfully over the years ahead.
personalizeWizard.savingsInsightHasValue: Including about {{amount}} elsewhere helps us show a fuller retirement picture. At typical growth assumptions, extra savings can materially boost your ending balance — we'll reflect this in your outlook.
personalizeWizard.savingsSuggestSubtitle: Many participants enter a rough rollover or savings estimate — you can update later.
personalizeWizard.comfortQuestion: How comfortable are you with investment risk?
personalizeWizard.comfortSubtitle: This helps us recommend a portfolio that matches your style.
personalizeWizard.comfortOptConservativeDesc: Lower risk, emphasis on preserving what you've built.
personalizeWizard.comfortOptBalancedDesc: Moderate growth with a mix of stocks and steadier assets.
personalizeWizard.comfortOptGrowthDesc: Higher growth potential with more market movement.
personalizeWizard.comfortOptAggressiveDesc: Maximum growth focus; expect sharper swings.
personalizeWizard.comfortSuggestTitle: Balanced is the most common choice
personalizeWizard.comfortSuggestSubtitle: Based on similar participants in your plan
personalizeWizard.comfortInsightConservative: Conservative options prioritize stability — many near-retirees choose this to sleep better when markets swing.
personalizeWizard.comfortInsightBalanced: Balanced portfolios are chosen by about 60% of users — a middle path between growth and steadiness.
personalizeWizard.comfortInsightGrowth: Growth-oriented mixes aim for higher long-term returns while accepting more ups and downs along the way.
personalizeWizard.comfortInsightAggressive: Aggressive strategies chase maximum growth; they suit long time horizons and higher tolerance for volatility.
personalizeWizard.comfortReturnsConservative: Historically, conservative mixes have aimed for steadier, lower-volatility outcomes over long periods.
personalizeWizard.comfortReturnsBalanced: A balanced approach has often targeted moderate long-term growth with managed swings.
personalizeWizard.comfortReturnsGrowth: Growth-oriented strategies typically seek higher long-term returns with more market movement.
personalizeWizard.comfortReturnsAggressive: Aggressive portfolios may offer higher return potential over decades, with deeper drawdowns possible.
personalizeWizard.locationSubtitle: Your location helps us estimate cost of living and plan smarter.
personalizeWizard.locationSearchAria: Search U.S. states and District of Columbia
personalizeWizard.locationSearchHint: All 50 states and D.C. — type to narrow the list, or scroll to pick one.
personalizeWizard.locationSuggestSubtitle: Many participants start with a Sun Belt state — you can change this anytime.
personalizeWizard.locationInsightFallback: Choose a state, try a popular pick, or select "Not sure yet" — you can refine this later.
personalizeWizard.featuredFloridaDesc: Warm weather, beaches, and retiree-friendly communities.
personalizeWizard.featuredArizonaDesc: Dry climate, golf, and strong snowbird appeal.
personalizeWizard.featuredNorthCarolinaDesc: Mountains to coast with a moderate cost of living.
personalizeWizard.featuredSouthCarolinaDesc: Coastal charm and lower taxes than many peers.

stateInsights.Florida: Florida has no state income tax and a lower cost of living than many coastal states — a popular pick for stretching retirement dollars.
stateInsights.Arizona: Arizona draws retirees for sunshine and outdoor living; housing costs vary by city but many areas remain affordable.
stateInsights.North_Carolina: North Carolina blends mild seasons and diverse regions, often landing in the sweet spot for taxes and lifestyle.
stateInsights.South_Carolina: South Carolina offers coastal living and generally lower property taxes than the national average in many counties.
stateInsights.California: California’s cost of living runs high in many metros, but mild climate and services appeal to retirees — we’ll stress-test your plan for West Coast expenses.
stateInsights.Texas: Texas has no state income tax on wages; property taxes can be meaningful depending on county — we’ll reflect both in your projections.
stateInsights.Nevada: Nevada has no state income tax on wages and draws retirees to Las Vegas and Reno — desert living with varied housing costs.
stateInsights.Washington: Washington has no state income tax on wages, though housing costs in Seattle and nearby metros are often steep — we’ll balance tax savings with living costs.
stateInsights.Tennessee: Tennessee has no state income tax on wages and no tax on most retirement income — a growing retiree hub with moderate COL outside major cities.
stateInsights.New_York: New York combines high services with high costs in downstate metros; upstate can be more affordable — your choice shapes income and tax assumptions.
stateInsights.Illinois: Illinois leans on property taxes in many communities; Chicago-area costs differ sharply from downstate — we’ll align estimates with your destination.
stateInsights.Pennsylvania: Pennsylvania exempts most retirement income for seniors in many cases and offers urban-to-rural variety — we’ll tune withdrawals to local norms.
stateInsights.Colorado: Colorado balances outdoor lifestyle with growing Front Range costs; tax treatment of retirement income has nuances we’ll factor in.
stateInsights.Georgia: Georgia offers a warm climate and retiree-friendly retirement-income exclusions up to limits — Atlanta’s costs differ from smaller towns.
stateInsights.Michigan: Michigan mixes affordable smaller cities with seasonal appeal; Great Lakes living can mean lower housing costs than national coastal peers.
stateInsights.Ohio: Ohio often lands below average on living costs, with a mix of metros and college towns — a practical Midwest retirement base.
stateInsights.Virginia: Virginia balances proximity to D.C. with mountain and coastal options; state tax on retirement income depends on age and sources — we’ll use typical assumptions.
stateInsights.Oregon: Oregon has no sales tax but income tax on most retirement withdrawals — Pacific Northwest appeal with a different tax trade-off than neighbors.
stateInsights.Massachusetts: Massachusetts combines strong healthcare access with higher living costs in Greater Boston — we’ll stress income needs for New England retirement.
stateInsights.New_Jersey: New Jersey has high property taxes in many towns but excludes much Social Security from state tax — we’ll weigh taxes against proximity to NYC and Philly.
stateInsights.Maryland: Maryland offers Chesapeake and mountain living with income taxes that include retirement income — DC suburbs tend to be the priciest slice.
stateInsights.Wisconsin: Wisconsin’s cost of living is moderate in many markets, with cold winters offset by lower housing costs than coastal states.
stateInsights.Minnesota: Minnesota taxes retirement income but offers strong services; Twin Cities costs exceed many rural areas — we’ll align with your intended community.
stateInsights.Louisiana: Louisiana keeps overall living costs relatively low; Gulf Coast humidity and hurricane risk are lifestyle factors retirees weigh.
stateInsights.Missouri: Missouri combines low average living costs with four-season weather and major metros like Kansas City and St. Louis.
stateInsights.Indiana: Indiana is often cited as affordable Midwest living, with modest taxes and Indianapolis as the economic hub.
stateInsights.Kentucky: Kentucky taxes some retirement income but offers a lower overall cost profile than many coastal alternatives.
stateInsights.Alabama: Alabama features warm weather and a low cost of living in much of the state — Gulf and college-town markets vary.
stateInsights.Mississippi: Mississippi ranks among the lowest cost-of-living states — we’ll still model healthcare and long-term care assumptions carefully.
stateInsights.Arkansas: Arkansas offers Ozarks and Delta living with generally affordable housing — a quiet retirement option for many.
stateInsights.Oklahoma: Oklahoma combines low living costs with plains and city options; tornado risk is a regional consideration.
stateInsights.Iowa: Iowa balances agricultural affordability with solid healthcare access in its larger cities.
stateInsights.Nebraska: Nebraska offers Midwest stability; Omaha and Lincoln carry higher costs than rural towns.
stateInsights.Kansas: Kansas provides affordable plains living with Wichita and KC suburbs as pricier pockets.
stateInsights.Delaware: Delaware has no sales tax and relatively low property taxes in many areas — a small state with beach and corporate corridors.
stateInsights.Connecticut: Connecticut has high living costs along the Gold Coast but offers New England charm and proximity to NYC and Boston.
stateInsights.Rhode_Island: Rhode Island packs coast and culture into a small footprint — costs run higher than national averages.
stateInsights.Vermont: Vermont appeals for scenery and small-town life; housing and winter costs can surprise newcomers.
stateInsights.New_Mexico: New Mexico mixes high desert and mountain towns with moderate costs outside Santa Fe and Albuquerque hotspots.
stateInsights.Utah: Utah offers outdoor access and fast-growing metros; Wasatch Front housing has climbed — we’ll reflect mountain-West trends.
stateInsights.Idaho: Idaho has drawn retirees for affordability, though Boise-area growth has pushed prices up — still often below West Coast peers.
stateInsights.Montana: Montana’s wide-open spaces appeal to nature-focused retirees; some towns have seen sharp housing growth.
stateInsights.Wyoming: Wyoming has no state income tax on wages and low population density — wind and winter are part of the lifestyle trade-off.
stateInsights.Alaska: Alaska has no state income tax and a unique remote cost profile — energy and goods often cost more than in the lower 48.
stateInsights.Hawaii: Hawaii’s paradise premium shows up in housing and goods — we’ll assume higher spending needs for island retirement.
stateInsights.District_of_Columbia: D.C. offers urban amenities and healthcare access with a high cost of living — we’ll model accordingly for city retirement.
stateInsights.Maine: Maine combines coastline and forests with seasonal tourism swings — Portland costs exceed many rural towns.
stateInsights.West_Virginia: West Virginia offers Appalachian affordability and outdoor recreation — smaller economies mean weighing healthcare access.
stateInsights.North_Dakota: North Dakota provides low-density, affordable living with cold winters — energy-sector towns can differ from farm communities.
stateInsights.South_Dakota: South Dakota has no state income tax on wages and a low overall cost profile — Rapid City and Sioux Falls anchor retiree options.
stateInsights._noWageTax: {{state}} does not levy state income tax on wages like most states — that can help retirement cash flow. We'll reflect that in your projections.
stateInsights._generic: {{state}} has its own cost-of-living and tax profile. Your choice helps us tune retirement income, tax, and withdrawal estimates for that state.
